name: Memory Tiering in a Python Virtual Machine
summary: Li et al.'s Memory Tiering in Python Virtual Machine (VMIL 2025) explores adapting a CPython-like runtime to heterogeneous memory. Memory tiering moves data among memory classes with different latency, bandwidth, capacity, or persistence characteristics based on runtime hotness and object behaviour.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Li et al.'s **Memory Tiering in Python Virtual Machine** (VMIL 2025) explores adapting a CPython-like runtime to heterogeneous memory.

Memory tiering moves data among memory classes with different latency, bandwidth, capacity, or persistence characteristics — for example DRAM plus slower large-capacity memory — based on runtime hotness and object behaviour.

sections.title: VM-level vs OS-level tiering
sections.content:
The VM-level problem differs from OS-only page migration. A language runtime knows object types, allocation sites, container layouts, reference relationships, and interpreter hot paths; using that semantic information can guide placement better than page-level recency alone.

In a Python VM, the challenge is especially visible because **object overhead is high, pointer chasing is common, and container objects create rich graph structure**. CPython's `PyObject` header per value is the canonical example: a typical small-int or string occupies tens of bytes of header before any payload, and tiering decisions can move cold-but-rooted PyObjects to slower memory without a measurable performance cost.

sections.title: Placement policy alongside reclamation policy
sections.content:
The design lesson: **future memory managers may need placement policy, not just reclamation policy**. Heap design can expose hot/cold object classification, allocation-site metadata, and profiling hooks so runtimes can target heterogeneous memory without rewriting user programs.

This is adjacent to allocators (which decide where to allocate within a single tier), GC layout (which decides how objects are organised on the heap), and tracing/profiling (because tiering decisions require runtime telemetry).

Status (as of 2026-04): research-grade, VMIL 2025 artifact.

sources.label: Memory Tiering in Python Virtual Machine (VMIL 2025)
